expanded_description: A clear introduction to virus structure for secondary school biology. This covers what viruses are made of—genetic material (DNA or RNA), a protein coat (capsid), and sometimes an envelope—and how their structure determines which cells they can infect and how they spread.

core_concept.principle: Viruses are tiny infectious particles made of genetic material (DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protein coat called a capsid. Some viruses also have an outer envelope made of fat. They are not alive—they cannot reproduce on their own and must infect living cells to make more viruses.
core_concept.explanation:
Viruses are the smallest infectious agents. Most are so tiny that you cannot see them with a regular microscope; you need an electron microscope. They are not cells. They have no nucleus, no organelles, no way to make energy or proteins. They are essentially a package of genetic material (DNA or RNA) wrapped in a protein coat.

The genetic material (genome) can be DNA or RNA. It can be double-stranded or single-stranded. It can be one piece or several pieces. The type of genetic material determines how the virus reproduces.

The capsid is the protein shell that protects the genetic material. Capsids are made of many identical protein subunits called capsomeres. These subunits self-assemble into precise shapes. Most viruses have either icosahedral symmetry (like a soccer ball) or helical symmetry (like a spiral staircase). Some complex viruses (like bacteriophages) have a combination of shapes.

Some viruses have an envelope. This is a layer of fat (lipid) that they steal from the host cell when they bud out. The envelope is studded with viral proteins called spikes or glycoproteins. These spikes help the virus attach to and enter host cells. Enveloped viruses are more fragile—they are killed by soap, alcohol, and drying out. Non-enveloped (naked) viruses are tougher—they can survive on surfaces for days.

Viral structure determines what cells a virus can infect. The spikes on the surface must fit into receptors on the host cell like a key in a lock. If the spikes do not match the receptors, the virus cannot get in. This is why most viruses infect only specific types of cells—HIV infects immune cells, influenza infects lung cells, and so on.

Understanding virus structure helps us understand how viruses spread, how to kill them (with soap, alcohol, heat), and how to make vaccines. The spike proteins are often the target of vaccines—they are what your immune system learns to recognize.

image.alt: A diagram showing a virus with genetic material (DNA/RNA), capsid (protein coat), and envelope with spikes
image.caption: Virus structure: Genetic material (DNA or RNA) inside a protein capsid. Some viruses have an envelope (fat layer) with spikes (glycoproteins). Non-enveloped (naked) viruses lack the envelope.

sections.introduction:
Viruses are the ultimate parasites. They are not alive—they cannot reproduce, grow, or carry out metabolism on their own. But they are not just inert chemicals either. They have genetic material, they evolve, and they cause diseases that have shaped human history.

Viruses were discovered in the late 1800s. Scientists found that something smaller than bacteria could cause disease. They called it a "filterable virus" because it passed through filters that trapped bacteria. In the 1930s, the electron microscope let scientists see viruses for the first time. They saw tiny particles with regular shapes—some like soccer balls, some like spirals, some like tiny spacecraft.

Viruses are everywhere. They infect bacteria, plants, animals, and humans. There are more viruses on Earth than all other life forms combined. A liter of seawater contains about 10 billion viruses. They are a major force in evolution—they transfer genes between organisms and kill huge numbers of bacteria every day.

Viruses cause many diseases: the common cold, influenza, COVID-19, HIV/AIDS, Ebola, measles, chickenpox, and many more. But viruses are not all bad. Some viruses kill bacteria (bacteriophages) and are being used to treat antibiotic-resistant infections. Some viruses are used in gene therapy to deliver healthy genes to patients. And our own DNA is full of viral genes that were incorporated millions of years ago.

To understand viruses, you have to understand their structure. The structure tells you how they infect cells, how they spread, and how to stop them. A virus is like a tiny machine—every part has a job. This section will look at the parts and how they work.

introduction_image.alt: A timeline of virus discovery: filterable agents, electron microscope images, modern cryo-EM structures
introduction_image.caption: Virus discovery: 1890s (filterable agents), 1930s (first EM images), 1980s (HIV identified), 2020s (cryo-EM structures of SARS-CoV-2). Each advance revealed more about viral structure.

key_concept.definition: Viruses are acellular particles made of genetic material (DNA or RNA) enclosed in a protein capsid. Some have an envelope of fat. They cannot reproduce on their own and must infect host cells to make new viruses.
key_concept.context: Viruses range in size from 20 nm (parvoviruses) to 1.5 µm (pandoraviruses). Most are 20-300 nm. They are much smaller than bacteria. Their genetic material can be DNA or RNA, double-stranded or single-stranded. The capsid is made of repeating protein subunits (capsomeres) that self-assemble. The envelope (if present) is derived from the host cell membrane and contains viral spike proteins. Structure determines host range, transmission, and sensitivity to disinfectants.

image.alt: Size comparison: parvovirus (20 nm), influenza (100 nm), HIV (100 nm), bacteria (1000 nm), human cell (10,000 nm)
image.caption: Virus sizes: Most viruses are 20-300 nm. They are 10-100 times smaller than bacteria and 1000 times smaller than human cells.

sections.key_concept: Viruses are made of three basic parts: genetic material (DNA or RNA), a protein capsid that protects it, and sometimes an envelope that helps them enter cells. The structure determines what cells they can infect and how they spread.

classifications.type: Viral Genetic Material: DNA or RNA
classifications.value: Understanding viral genomes explains how viruses reproduce and why some mutate faster than others.
characteristics: DNA viruses: Have DNA as genetic material. Can be double-stranded (dsDNA) or single-stranded (ssDNA). Examples: herpesvirus, smallpox, adenovirus.
RNA viruses: Have RNA as genetic material. Can be double-stranded (dsRNA) or single-stranded (ssRNA). Most human viruses are RNA viruses. Examples: influenza, HIV, coronavirus, Ebola.
Positive-sense RNA (+ssRNA): The RNA can be used directly as a message to make proteins. Examples: coronavirus, poliovirus, hepatitis C.
Negative-sense RNA (-ssRNA): The RNA must first be copied into positive-sense RNA before proteins can be made. Examples: influenza, Ebola, rabies.
Segmented genomes: Some viruses have their genome in multiple pieces. Example: influenza has 8 RNA segments. This allows them to swap pieces when two viruses infect the same cell (reassortment).
classifications.behavior:
The type of genetic material determines how a virus reproduces. DNA viruses often use the host cell's machinery to copy their DNA. They tend to have lower mutation rates because host DNA polymerases have proofreading. DNA viruses often cause persistent infections (like herpes) because they can hide in the nucleus.

RNA viruses use their own enzymes (RNA polymerases) to copy their RNA. These enzymes make mistakes—they have no proofreading. This means RNA viruses mutate very quickly. This is why you need a new flu shot every year—the virus is constantly changing.

Positive-sense RNA viruses are ready to go. Their RNA acts like a message the cell can read immediately. Negative-sense RNA viruses must carry their own polymerase enzyme to make messages. This makes them slower to start but also gives them more control.

Segmented genomes are like having the instruction book in separate chapters. If two different viruses infect the same cell, they can swap chapters. This is called reassortment. It can create new viruses that are very different from the parents. This is how new pandemic flu strains arise.

examples.process: How Influenza Changes: Drift and Shift
examples.explanation:
Influenza virus has 8 RNA segments. It is an RNA virus, so it mutates quickly. There are two ways it changes.

Antigenic drift: Small changes in the genes happen over time as the virus copies its RNA. These changes make the virus slightly different from previous strains. This is why we need new flu vaccines every year—the virus has drifted enough that last year's vaccine may not work well.

Antigenic shift: If two different flu viruses infect the same cell, they can swap RNA segments. This can create a completely new virus. For example, a bird flu virus and a human flu virus could swap segments, creating a new virus that humans have never seen before. This is how pandemics start. The 2009 H1N1 pandemic was caused by a virus that had swapped segments between human, bird, and swine flu viruses.

This ability to change makes influenza a constant threat.

image.alt: Influenza antigenic drift (small changes) and antigenic shift (reassortment of segments)
image.caption: Influenza changes: Antigenic drift (point mutations) causes gradual change. Antigenic shift (reassortment of RNA segments) can create new pandemic strains.

classifications.value: Understanding the capsid explains how the viral genome is protected and how the virus attaches to cells.
characteristics: Capsid: The protein shell that encloses the viral genome. Made of many identical protein subunits called capsomeres.
Icosahedral symmetry: Soccer ball shape. 20 triangular faces, 12 corners. Most efficient way to enclose space with identical parts. Examples: poliovirus, adenovirus, herpesvirus.
Helical symmetry: Spiral shape. Capsid proteins wrap around the genetic material like a spiral staircase. Examples: influenza virus, Ebola virus, tobacco mosaic virus.
Complex symmetry: Other shapes. Bacteriophages have icosahedral heads and helical tails. Poxviruses are brick-shaped.
Capsid functions: Protects the genetic material, helps the virus attach to cells, and in some viruses, helps release the genome into the cell.
classifications.behavior:
The capsid is like a suitcase for the viral genome. It is made of many copies of just a few proteins. This is efficient—the virus does not need a different gene for every part of the capsid. The proteins self-assemble into the correct shape without needing extra instructions.

Icosahedral capsids are the most common. Think of a soccer ball. It has 20 hexagons and 12 pentagons. Viral capsids work the same way. The number of protein subunits is determined by the T-number. T=1 has 60 subunits; T=3 has 180; T=7 has 420. Larger T-numbers can hold larger genomes.

Helical capsids are like a tube. The length of the tube depends on the size of the genome. The width is fixed by how the proteins fit together. Tobacco mosaic virus is a classic example—it is a long, thin rod made of RNA spiraling through protein subunits.

Complex viruses have more complicated shapes. Bacteriophages (viruses that infect bacteria) look like tiny spacecraft. They have an icosahedral head that holds the DNA, a tail, and tail fibers that recognize the bacteria. When they find a suitable bacterium, they inject their DNA like a syringe.

examples.explanation:
Bacteriophages (phages) are viruses that infect bacteria. They have a complex structure that is optimized for injecting DNA into bacteria.

The head is icosahedral and contains the DNA. The tail is a hollow tube with a sheath that can contract. The baseplate has tail fibers that recognize the bacterial surface.

When a phage finds a suitable bacterium, it attaches with its tail fibers. The baseplate locks onto the bacterial surface. The tail sheath contracts, driving the hollow tail tube through the bacterial cell wall. The DNA is injected from the head through the tail tube into the bacterium. The empty phage head remains outside.

Inside the bacterium, the viral DNA takes over. It uses the bacterium's machinery to make new phage proteins and copies of the DNA. Eventually, the bacterium bursts (lyses), releasing hundreds of new phages.

Phages are being used to treat antibiotic-resistant bacterial infections. They are very specific—each phage infects only certain bacteria, so they do not harm human cells or good bacteria.

image.alt: Bacteriophage structure: icosahedral head, tail sheath, baseplate, tail fibers
image.caption: Bacteriophage: Icosahedral head (DNA), tail sheath, baseplate, tail fibers. Tail fibers recognize bacteria; sheath contracts to inject DNA. Used to treat antibiotic-resistant infections.

classifications.value: Understanding the envelope explains why some viruses are fragile and how they enter cells.
characteristics: Envelope: A layer of fat (lipid) that surrounds the capsid. Taken from the host cell membrane when the virus buds out.
Glycoproteins: Proteins on the envelope surface that stick out like spikes. They help the virus attach to and enter host cells.
Enveloped viruses: Examples: influenza, HIV, coronavirus, herpesvirus.
Non-enveloped (naked) viruses: No envelope. Examples: poliovirus, norovirus, adenovirus.
Envelope sensitivity: Enveloped viruses are fragile—they are killed by soap, alcohol, drying, and heat. Non-enveloped viruses are tough—they can survive on surfaces for days and resist alcohol.
classifications.behavior:
The envelope is not made by the virus. The virus steals it from the host cell. As the virus buds out of the cell, it wraps itself in a piece of the cell's membrane. The viral spikes are embedded in this membrane.

The spikes are the virus's keys. They recognize specific proteins (receptors) on the surface of target cells. The spike must fit the receptor like a key in a lock. This is why viruses only infect certain cells. HIV's spike binds to CD4 on immune cells. Influenza's spike binds to sialic acid on lung cells. SARS-CoV-2's spike binds to ACE2 on many cell types.

After binding, the envelope helps the virus enter the cell. The virus fuses its envelope with the cell's membrane, releasing the capsid inside. Some viruses are taken into the cell in a vesicle, then the envelope fuses with the vesicle membrane.

Because the envelope is made of fat, it is fragile. Soap breaks down fat, so soap kills enveloped viruses. Alcohol also disrupts the envelope. Enveloped viruses do not survive long on surfaces—they dry out and die. Non-enveloped viruses have a tough protein coat and can survive for days or weeks on surfaces. This is why norovirus (non-enveloped) spreads so easily—it can survive on doorknobs and countertops for a long time.

examples.explanation:
The spike protein of SARS-CoV-2 (the virus that causes COVID-19) is the key that lets the virus into cells. It binds to a protein called ACE2 on the surface of human cells.

The spike is a trimer—three identical proteins stuck together. At the tip of each spike is the receptor-binding domain (RBD). The RBD swings up and down. When it swings up, it can bind to ACE2.

After binding, the spike changes shape. It pulls the virus envelope and the cell membrane together, causing them to fuse. The viral RNA is released into the cell.

Because the spike is on the surface, it is the main target of the immune system. Antibodies that block the spike can prevent infection. This is why COVID-19 vaccines are designed to teach your body to recognize the spike protein.

The spike also changes over time. New variants have mutations in the spike that make it bind tighter to ACE2 or help it escape antibodies. This is why new variants keep appearing.

image.alt: SARS-CoV-2 spike protein structure showing RBD binding to ACE2 and membrane fusion
image.caption: Spike protein: Binds ACE2 receptor on human cells → changes shape → fuses virus and cell membranes → releases viral RNA. Target of vaccines and antibodies.

principles.statement: The spikes on a virus determine what cells it can infect. They must fit into receptors on the host cell like a key in a lock. If they do not fit, the virus cannot get in.
principles.application:
Viruses are picky about what cells they infect. HIV infects only cells with CD4 receptors (immune cells). Influenza infects cells with sialic acid receptors (lung cells). SARS-CoV-2 infects cells with ACE2 receptors (many cell types, especially in lungs).

This specificity is determined by the shape of the viral spike and the shape of the host receptor. They have to fit together. Small changes in the spike can change what receptors it binds to. This is how viruses jump between species. A bird flu virus may evolve a spike that can bind to human receptors, allowing it to infect people.

Receptors are also found in different parts of the body. HIV's receptor is on immune cells, so HIV attacks the immune system. Influenza's receptor is on lung cells, so it causes respiratory disease. The location of the receptor determines the symptoms.

This principle is why we can make vaccines. The immune system learns to recognize the spike. If you are vaccinated, your body can block the spike before it can bind to receptors.
principles.example: Influenza virus normally infects birds. Bird flu viruses have spikes that bind to sialic acid receptors found in bird intestines. To infect humans, the virus needs to change its spike so it can bind to human sialic acid receptors (found in the lungs). This is why bird flu rarely infects humans—the spike does not fit. When it does change, it can cause a pandemic because humans have no immunity to the new spike.
principles.analogy: The spike is like a key. The receptor is like a lock. If the key fits the lock, the door opens. If it does not fit, you cannot get in. Viruses have evolved keys that fit specific locks on specific cells.

image.alt: Diagram showing viral spikes binding to cell receptors: HIV to CD4, influenza to sialic acid, SARS-CoV-2 to ACE2
image.caption: Host range: Viral spikes must fit host cell receptors. HIV spike binds CD4 (immune cells); influenza spike binds sialic acid (lung cells); SARS-CoV-2 spike binds ACE2 (many cells).

principles.principle: Enveloped Viruses Are Fragile; Naked Viruses Are Tough
principles.statement: The envelope is made of fat, which is easily broken by soap, alcohol, and drying. Non-enveloped (naked) viruses have a tough protein coat and can survive longer in the environment.
principles.application:
Enveloped viruses (influenza, HIV, coronavirus) are sensitive to disinfectants. Soap breaks down the fat envelope, killing the virus. Alcohol also disrupts the envelope. This is why washing your hands with soap is so effective against COVID-19. Enveloped viruses also dry out quickly on surfaces—they survive only a few hours to a few days.

Non-enveloped viruses (norovirus, poliovirus) are much tougher. They can survive on surfaces for days or weeks. Alcohol does not kill them—you need bleach or other strong disinfectants. They can also survive stomach acid, which is why they often spread through contaminated food or water.

This difference affects how we control outbreaks. For influenza and COVID-19, hand washing and surface cleaning are important, but the main route is respiratory droplets. For norovirus, you need to disinfect surfaces with bleach and wash hands thoroughly with soap and water—alcohol sanitizers are not enough.
principles.example: Norovirus causes stomach flu. It is non-enveloped, so it survives on surfaces for days. It is also resistant to alcohol. This is why norovirus outbreaks often occur on cruise ships—the virus spreads easily and is hard to kill. To stop it, you need to use bleach or other strong disinfectants and wash hands thoroughly with soap and water. Alcohol hand sanitizers are not effective against norovirus.
principles.analogy: Think of the envelope as a raincoat. Enveloped viruses wear a raincoat (fat layer) that helps them get into cells but is easily damaged. Non-enveloped viruses go naked—they have a tough protein coat that can withstand harsh conditions but makes entry more difficult. Each strategy has advantages and disadvantages.

image.alt: Comparison of enveloped virus (fragile, killed by soap) and non-enveloped virus (tough, survives on surfaces)
image.caption: Envelope stability: Enveloped viruses (influenza, HIV) are fragile—killed by soap, alcohol, drying. Non-enveloped viruses (norovirus, poliovirus) are tough—survive on surfaces, resist alcohol.

principles.statement: The capsid can only hold so much genetic material. Viruses with icosahedral capsids have a maximum genome size. This limits how complex a virus can be.
principles.application:
Icosahedral capsids have a fixed volume. The larger the capsid, the larger the genome it can hold. But the capsid proteins have to assemble correctly, and larger capsids require more protein subunits. There is a limit to how big an icosahedral virus can be. Most icosahedral viruses have genomes under 200,000 bases.

Helical capsids can be any length, but the width is fixed. This allows them to have longer genomes. Some helical viruses have genomes over 300,000 bases.

Complex viruses like poxviruses have brick-shaped structures that can hold very large genomes. Poxviruses have genomes over 200,000 bases and encode many proteins, including proteins that help them evade the immune system.

Giant viruses (like pandoraviruses) have been discovered recently. They are larger than some bacteria and have genomes over 2 million bases. They have capsid structures that are not simple icosahedrons or helices. They challenge the idea that viruses are simple.
principles.example:
Poliovirus is tiny—only 30 nm across. Its capsid is icosahedral with a T=3 symmetry. It holds only about 7,500 bases of RNA. It has only 11 genes. It must use host cell machinery for almost everything.

Poxviruses (like smallpox) are much larger—300 nm across. They have complex brick-shaped capsids. Their genomes are about 200,000 bases, encoding over 200 proteins. They carry many of their own replication enzymes, making them more independent of the host.

Giant viruses (like Pandoravirus) are 1,000 nm long—visible under a light microscope. Their genomes are over 2 million bases, larger than some bacteria. They encode proteins for translation, which was thought to be unique to cells. They blur the line between viruses and cells.
principles.analogy: Capsids are like suitcases. A small suitcase (icosahedral) can only hold a few clothes (genes). A duffel bag (helical) can hold more, but it is a different shape. A trunk (complex) can hold even more. Giant viruses are like steamer trunks—they hold so much that they challenge what we think a virus should be.

image.alt: Comparison of capsid sizes and genome sizes: poliovirus (small), influenza (medium), poxvirus (large), pandoravirus (giant)
image.caption: Capsid and genome size: Icosahedral viruses (poliovirus) have small genomes. Helical viruses (influenza) can have larger genomes. Complex viruses (poxvirus) have large genomes. Giant viruses (pandoravirus) challenge virus definitions.

components.definition: A virus particle (virion) has three main parts: the genome (DNA or RNA), the capsid (protein shell), and sometimes an envelope (fat layer). These parts work together to protect the genome and deliver it into host cells.
components.interpretation: For a virus to be infectious, its genome must be intact, its capsid must protect the genome, its envelope (if present) must be functional, and its spikes must be able to bind to host receptors. If any part is damaged, the virus cannot infect.

image.alt: A diagram of a virus with labeled parts: genome, capsid, envelope, spike proteins
image.caption: Viral components: Genome (genetic instructions), Capsid (protection), Envelope (entry), Spikes (attachment). All parts work together.

categories.description: Enveloped viruses steal their envelope from the host cell. They bud out through the cell membrane, wrapping themselves in a piece of it.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
When an enveloped virus is made inside a cell, the viral spikes are inserted into the cell's membrane. The capsid (with the genome inside) moves to the membrane. The capsid pushes against the membrane, wrapping itself in a piece of it. The membrane pinches off, releasing a virus particle wrapped in the cell's membrane. This is called budding.

The virus does not just take any membrane. The spikes are concentrated in certain areas of the membrane (lipid rafts). The virus buds from these areas, ensuring it has many spikes on its surface.

Some viruses bud from the cell surface (influenza, HIV). Others bud from internal membranes like the endoplasmic reticulum or Golgi apparatus (coronaviruses). The envelope's composition reflects the membrane it came from.

Because the envelope is stolen from the host cell, it contains some host proteins. This can help the virus hide from the immune system—it looks a bit like the host.

examples.explanation:
HIV makes its proteins in the infected cell. The envelope spike proteins (gp120 and gp41) are made in the endoplasmic reticulum, processed in the Golgi, and sent to the cell surface. They cluster in areas called lipid rafts.

The capsid proteins (Gag) assemble into a sphere inside the cell. The Gag proteins have a tail that binds to the spike proteins. The assembled capsid moves to the cell surface and pushes against the membrane. The membrane wraps around it, and the virus pinches off. This process uses the cell's own machinery (ESCRT) to cut the membrane.

The new virus is now wrapped in a piece of the host cell's membrane. It has host proteins on its surface, which can help it evade the immune system. It also has the viral spikes that will allow it to infect new cells.

HIV drugs called protease inhibitors block the maturation of the capsid, preventing the virus from becoming infectious.

image.alt: HIV budding from a cell: capsid assembly, membrane wrapping, release
image.caption: HIV budding: Capsid assembles under membrane → membrane wraps around → virus pinches off → new virion with envelope and spikes. Host ESCRT machinery helps cut the membrane.

categories.description: Scientists use cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) to see viruses at near-atomic resolution. This helps us understand how they work and how to stop them.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Cryo-EM is a way to see tiny structures like viruses. First, the viruses are frozen rapidly in a thin layer of ice. This preserves them in their natural state—no stains or chemicals. Then, an electron beam is shot through the sample. The electrons scatter off the virus, creating an image. Thousands of images are combined to create a 3D model.

This technique has revolutionized virology. Before cryo-EM, scientists had to grow crystals of viral proteins to see their structure. Many viruses could not be crystallized. Cryo-EM lets us see whole viruses, not just individual proteins. It can show how the spikes move, how the capsid is built, and how antibodies bind.

During the COVID-19 pandemic, cryo-EM was used to solve the structure of the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein in weeks. This allowed scientists to design vaccines and antibodies that target the spike. Without cryo-EM, this would have taken years.

examples.explanation:
In early 2020, scientists knew that SARS-CoV-2 used a spike protein to enter cells. They needed to know what the spike looked like to make a vaccine.

Using cryo-EM, they solved the spike structure in just weeks. They saw that the spike had three parts (a trimer) and that the receptor-binding domain (RBD) could swing up and down. They saw the shape of the spike in its prefusion state—before it fused with the cell.

This structure guided vaccine design. The mRNA vaccines (Pfizer, Moderna) were designed to make the spike protein in its prefusion form. This is the form that antibodies can best recognize. The structure also helped design monoclonal antibody treatments.

Cryo-EM continues to be used to study new variants. Scientists can see how mutations change the spike's shape and how they affect antibody binding.

image.alt: Cryo-EM image of SARS-CoV-2 spike protein and the resulting 3D structure
image.caption: Cryo-EM: Flash-frozen viruses imaged with electrons → thousands of images combined → 3D structure of spike protein. Guided COVID-19 vaccine design in weeks.

worked_examples.title: Classifying a New Virus from Experimental Data
worked_examples.problem: You isolate a new virus. Experiments show: (a) Electron microscopy shows spherical particles about 100 nm across with spikes on the surface; (b) Treatment with ether (which dissolves fat) destroys infectivity; (c) The virus's genome is RNA, but it is not infectious when extracted and added to cells alone; (d) The virus has eight pieces of RNA. (1) What kind of envelope does it have? (2) Is it positive-sense or negative-sense RNA? (3) How does it reproduce? (4) How would you disinfect surfaces contaminated with this virus?

step_by_step.calculation: Spherical shape with spikes suggests an enveloped virus. Ether treatment destroys infectivity → envelope is present (ether dissolves fat). This is an enveloped virus.

RNA genome is not infectious when extracted and added to cells alone → it cannot act as mRNA. This means it is negative-sense RNA. Negative-sense RNA must be transcribed into positive-sense RNA before proteins can be made. The virus must carry its own RNA polymerase to do this.
Eight pieces of RNA → segmented genome. This allows reassortment when two viruses infect the same cell. The virus is likely an influenza virus (which has 8 segments) or a related virus.

Enveloped virus → sensitive to soap and alcohol. Soap breaks down the fat envelope. Alcohol also disrupts the envelope. Hand washing with soap is effective. Alcohol-based hand sanitizers also work. The virus does not survive long on surfaces.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Enveloped virus. (2) Negative-sense RNA (genome not infectious alone). (3) Reproduces by making positive-sense RNA copies first; uses own RNA polymerase. (4) Soap, alcohol, or disinfectants that break down fat. This shows how structure tells us about virus biology and how to control it.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Virus structure reveals properties: Enveloped viruses are fragile (killed by soap). Negative-sense RNA viruses need to carry their own polymerase. Segmented genomes allow reassortment. These features guide treatment and prevention.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: Virus Assembly and Release
sections.introduction: Viruses are assembled inside host cells and then released. The process varies depending on whether the virus has an envelope.

process_flow.description: The virus uses the host cell to make viral proteins and copy the viral genome.
process_flow.mechanism: Viral genes are transcribed into mRNA. The mRNA is translated into viral proteins on host ribosomes. The viral genome is copied by viral enzymes (for RNA viruses) or host enzymes (for DNA viruses). Proteins and genomes accumulate in the cell.
process_flow.result: Viral proteins and genomes ready for assembly.
applications: Target for antivirals (block replication or protein synthesis)

image.alt: Viral proteins being made on ribosomes; viral genome being copied
image.caption: Step 1: Host cell makes viral proteins and copies viral genome. Proteins and genomes accumulate for assembly.

process_flow.description: Capsid proteins assemble around the viral genome, forming the nucleocapsid.
process_flow.mechanism: Capsid proteins self-assemble into a shell. In some viruses, the genome is packaged into an empty capsid. In others, the capsid assembles around the genome. The genome is specifically recognized by the capsid proteins so that host RNA is not packaged.

image.alt: Capsid proteins assembling around viral genome
image.caption: Step 2: Capsid proteins assemble around genome. Some viruses package genome into empty capsid; others assemble capsid around genome. Packaging signals ensure viral genome is selected.

process_flow.description: Enveloped viruses bud through host membranes; non-enveloped viruses lyse the cell or are released by exocytosis.
process_flow.mechanism: For enveloped viruses: Nucleocapsid moves to membrane where viral spikes are inserted. Membrane wraps around nucleocapsid and pinches off (budding), releasing the virus. The virus takes a piece of host membrane as its envelope. For non-enveloped viruses: The cell may burst (lysis), releasing viruses, or viruses may be released in vesicles.
process_flow.result: New viruses released; ready to infect new cells.
applications: Immune system can kill infected cells before viruses are released

image.alt: Virus budding through cell membrane; non-enveloped viruses causing cell lysis
image.caption: Step 3: Enveloped viruses bud through membranes, taking a piece of host membrane as envelope. Non-enveloped viruses often cause cell lysis. New viruses released.

sections.definition: Understanding virus structure has led to vaccines, antiviral drugs, and even treatments using viruses themselves.

process_types.energy_profile: Vaccines use viral structures (spike proteins, capsids) to train the immune system without causing disease.
process_types.direction: Vaccine (viral protein or weakened virus) → immune system makes antibodies and memory cells → protection against real virus
process_types.function: Prevent viral infections by preparing the immune system before exposure
goals: Induce neutralizing antibodies against viral spikes
Create memory B and T cells
process_types.energy_source: Immune system uses energy to make antibodies and memory cells; vaccines provide the blueprint.

primary_example.explanation:
The COVID-19 mRNA vaccines (Pfizer, Moderna) use the virus's structure to teach the immune system. The vaccines contain mRNA that codes for the spike protein. When injected, the mRNA enters cells, and the cells make the spike protein. The immune system sees the spike and makes antibodies and memory cells. If the person later encounters the real virus, the immune system is ready.

The spike protein in the vaccine is modified to stay in its prefusion shape. This shape is what antibodies best recognize. The structure of the spike was determined by cryo-EM, guiding the design.

These vaccines do not contain live virus, so they cannot cause COVID-19. They have been highly effective at preventing severe disease.

image.alt: mRNA vaccine mechanism: mRNA enters cell, makes spike protein, immune system responds
image.caption: mRNA vaccine: mRNA codes for spike protein → cell makes spike → immune system makes antibodies → protection. Spike structure from cryo-EM guided design.

image.alt: Different vaccine types: inactivated virus, subunit, mRNA, viral vector
image.caption: Vaccine types: Inactivated virus (whole virus killed), Subunit (spike protein only), mRNA (genetic instructions for spike), Viral vector (harmless virus carrying spike gene). All use viral structure to train immunity.

process_types.energy_profile: Antiviral drugs target viral proteins that are different from host proteins, so they block the virus without harming the host.
process_types.direction: Virus infection → drug blocks viral protein → virus cannot replicate
process_types.function: Treat viral infections by interfering with viral replication or entry
goals: Selective for viral proteins (minimize side effects)
process_types.energy_source: Drugs are small molecules that bind to viral proteins and block their function.

primary_example.explanation:
HIV makes a long polyprotein that must be cut into pieces by a viral enzyme called protease. Without cutting, the virus cannot assemble properly. Protease inhibitors are drugs that fit into the active site of the protease, blocking it. The polyprotein is not cut, and the virus makes non-infectious particles.

The structure of HIV protease was solved by X-ray crystallography. Scientists designed drugs to fit into the active site like a key in a lock. These drugs have turned HIV from a death sentence into a manageable chronic disease.

Protease inhibitors are also used to treat hepatitis C and are being developed for COVID-19.

image.alt: HIV protease structure with inhibitor bound in active site
image.caption: HIV protease inhibitor: Drug fits into active site, blocks enzyme. Viral polyprotein not cut, virus cannot assemble. Structure-based drug design revolutionized HIV treatment.

image.alt: Antiviral targets: entry inhibitors, polymerase inhibitors, protease inhibitors, integrase inhibitors
image.caption: Antiviral targets: Entry (block spikes), Polymerase (block replication), Protease (block processing), Integrase (block integration). Each targets a viral structure or enzyme.

sections.relationship: Vaccines and antiviral drugs both use our understanding of viral structure. Vaccines teach the immune system to recognize viral spikes. Antiviral drugs block viral proteins. Both are essential tools in fighting viral diseases.

sections.purpose: These are the essential vocabulary terms for understanding virus structure. Knowing these terms will help you describe how viruses are built and how they work.

activation.definition: The terms below cover the most important concepts in virus structure. Each term is defined simply, with context to help you remember its significance.
activation.effect: Mastering these terms will allow you to discuss viruses, their structure, and how they infect cells with confidence.

image.alt: A labeled diagram of a virus with key terms: genome, capsid, envelope, spike, capsomere
image.caption: Key virus terms: Knowing these structures helps you understand how viruses infect cells and how to stop them.

key_properties.explanation: A non-living infectious particle made of genetic material (DNA or RNA) enclosed in a protein capsid. Some have an envelope. Cannot reproduce without a host cell.
The protein shell that encloses the viral genome. Made of many copies of one or a few proteins. Protects the genome and helps the virus attach to cells.
The individual protein subunits that make up the capsid. Many identical capsomeres self-assemble into the capsid.
A layer of fat (lipid) surrounding the capsid in some viruses. Stolen from the host cell membrane. Contains viral spike proteins. Fragile—killed by soap and alcohol.
A protein on the surface of the virus (on the envelope or capsid) that attaches to host cell receptors. Key to host range. Target of vaccines and antibodies.
A shape like a soccer ball with 20 triangular faces. Efficient way to enclose space with identical protein subunits. Examples: poliovirus, adenovirus, herpesvirus.
A spiral shape. Capsid proteins wrap around the genome like a spiral staircase. Examples: influenza virus, Ebola virus, tobacco mosaic virus.
The genetic material of the virus. Can be DNA or RNA, double-stranded or single-stranded, one piece or segmented. Determines how the virus reproduces.
RNA that can be directly read as a message by host ribosomes. Examples: coronavirus, poliovirus, hepatitis C.
RNA that must be copied into positive-sense RNA before proteins can be made. The virus must carry its own polymerase. Examples: influenza, Ebola, rabies.
When two segmented viruses infect the same cell and swap segments. Can create new viruses with new properties. Example: influenza pandemics from reassortment.
The process by which enveloped viruses acquire their envelope. The virus pushes through the cell membrane, wrapping itself in a piece of it.
A virus that infects bacteria. Often have complex structure (icosahedral head, helical tail). Used to treat antibiotic-resistant infections.
A protein on the host cell surface that the virus binds to. The viral spike must fit the receptor like a key in a lock. Determines which cells the virus can infect.
A technique for seeing viruses at near-atomic resolution. Viruses are flash-frozen and imaged with electrons. Thousands of images are combined into 3D structures.
A vaccine that uses mRNA to instruct cells to make a viral protein (like the spike). The immune system learns to recognize the protein. Used for COVID-19.
A drug that targets viral proteins to block replication. Examples: protease inhibitors (HIV), polymerase inhibitors (remdesivir).

summary:
Viruses are tiny infectious particles made of genetic material (DNA or RNA) surrounded by a protein capsid. Some have an envelope (fat layer) stolen from host cells. They are not alive—they cannot reproduce on their own and must infect host cells to make more viruses.

Viral genetic material can be DNA or RNA, double-stranded or single-stranded. RNA viruses mutate quickly because their polymerases make errors. Segmented genomes (like influenza) allow reassortment, creating new viruses. Positive-sense RNA viruses can be read directly; negative-sense viruses must carry their own polymerase.

The capsid protects the genome and helps the virus attach to cells. Icosahedral capsids are soccer-ball shaped; helical capsids are spiral; complex viruses have mixed shapes. Capsids are made of many identical protein subunits (capsomeres) that self-assemble.

Enveloped viruses have a fat layer stolen from the host cell. They are fragile—killed by soap, alcohol, and drying. Their spikes help them enter cells. Non-enveloped viruses are tougher—they survive on surfaces and resist alcohol.

Viral spikes determine host range. They must fit into receptors on host cells like a key in a lock. This is why viruses infect specific cells. The spike is the main target of vaccines and antibodies.

Understanding virus structure has led to vaccines (including mRNA vaccines for COVID-19) and antiviral drugs (like HIV protease inhibitors). Cryo-electron microscopy lets scientists see viruses at near-atomic resolution, guiding drug and vaccine design.

Viruses are simple but powerful. Their structure—genome, capsid, envelope—determines how they spread, what they infect, and how to stop them.
